techniques.evidence_quote: The attackers had apparently discovered that this particular Kubernetes console—an administrative portal for cloud application management—wasn't password protected and could therefore be accessed by anyone.
one of the console's 'pods,' or storage containers, included login credentials for a broader Tesla Amazon Web Services cloud environment. This allowed them to burrow deeper.
the mining server also used a proxy server as an intermediary to mask it and make it less traceable.
The attack communications all happened over SSL web encryption to hide their content from security-monitoring tools.
The mining malware also communicated with the attacker's server on an unusual IP port, making it less likely that a port scanner would detect it as malicious.
one of the console's 'pods,' or storage containers, included login credentials for a broader Tesla Amazon Web Services cloud environment.
Tesla's compromised cloud platform also contained an S3 bucket that seemed to house sensitive proprietary data, like vehicle and mapping information and other instrument telemetry.
RedLock says the attackers obtained free proxying services and the SSL certificate from the internet infrastructure firm Cloudflare.
The attackers were running their own mining server, making it less likely that it would land on malware-scanner black lists.

some of Tesla's Amazon Web Services cloud infrastructure was running mining malware in a far-reaching and well-hidden cryptojacking campaign... deploying scripts to establish their cryptojacking operation, which was built on the popular Stratum bitcoin mining protocol.

attack_chain.description: Attackers discovered and accessed Tesla's Kubernetes administrative console that was exposed to the public internet with no password protection.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: The attackers had apparently discovered that this particular Kubernetes console—an administrative portal for cloud application management—wasn't password protected and could therefore be accessed by anyone.

attack_chain.description: Inside a Kubernetes pod, attackers found plaintext AWS credentials that granted access to Tesla's broader AWS cloud environment.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: one of the console's 'pods,' or storage containers, included login credentials for a broader Tesla Amazon Web Services cloud environment.

attack_chain.description: Using the harvested AWS credentials, attackers authenticated into Tesla's broader AWS environment to gain elevated cloud access.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: This allowed them to burrow deeper, deploying scripts to establish their cryptojacking operation.

attack_chain.description: Attackers deployed cryptocurrency mining scripts using the Stratum bitcoin mining protocol on Tesla's AWS compute infrastructure.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: deploying scripts to establish their cryptojacking operation, which was built on the popular Stratum bitcoin mining protocol.

attack_chain.description: The mining malware communicated with the attacker's C2 server on a non-standard port to evade port-scanner detection.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: The mining malware also communicated with the attacker's server on an unusual IP port, making it less likely that a port scanner would detect it as malicious.

attack_chain.description: All C2 communications were tunneled over SSL to hide content from security monitoring tools, with the SSL certificate obtained via Cloudflare.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: The attack communications all happened over SSL web encryption to hide their content from security-monitoring tools.

attack_chain.description: Attackers routed C2 traffic through a Cloudflare-based proxy server to mask the true location of their mining server and make it less traceable.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: the mining server also used a proxy server as an intermediary to mask it and make it less traceable. RedLock says the attackers obtained free proxying services and the SSL certificate from the internet infrastructure firm Cloudflare.

initial_access_vector: Unauthenticated Kubernetes administrative console exposed to the public internet with no password protection
persistence_mechanism: Deployed cryptocurrency mining scripts within Tesla's AWS compute environment via valid cloud credentials; mining ran indefinitely without noticeable impact due to the scale of Tesla's cloud usage
privilege_escalation: AWS credentials found in plaintext inside a Kubernetes pod were used to gain access to Tesla's broader AWS environment
